Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I tried to set aside specific times, but with 11 online courses happening simultaneously, I was constantly jumping between subjects without really mastering anything. I'd often study in bursts—sometimes for hours when panic set in, then nothing for days. I learn best by doing things hands-on, but most of my studying ended up being reactive rather than planned. I kept telling myself I'd get organized next week, but that week never really came.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd realize multiple deadlines were approaching and I'd scramble. I tried to create a schedule at the beginning of the semester, but managing so many courses made it impossible to stick to. Deadlines basically controlled my life—I was always in crisis mode rather than having a steady rhythm.
Q3: Usually it was panic that got me to log in—either a deadline notification or realizing I hadn't checked something in a while and feeling guilty about it. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which honestly made my anxiety worse most of the time. The gamified task system they introduced actually helped a bit because earning points gave me a small sense of accomplishment. But mostly, fear of falling further behind was my main motivator.
Q4: I wish I could say it became routine, but it never really did. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I logged in daily, but then something would overwhelm me and I'd avoid it entirely. The gamified elements made it slightly more appealing—like checking in to see my progress felt less daunting than facing a wall of assignments. But with my anxiety levels, even opening the LMS sometimes felt like too much to handle.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually helpful when I engaged with them because I could see how other students were thinking about the material. I also found the video content useful, even though I didn't watch as much as I should have—around 40 minutes total, which is embarrassing. When I did watch, I liked being able to pause and rewind since I learn better by doing things step by step. The gamified tasks gave me small wins that kept me going when everything else felt overwhelming.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources because I simply didn't have time with so many courses. The longer videos I'd often skip or skim through because sitting still for extended periods doesn't work well for how I learn. I also delayed discussion posts until the last minute because contributing felt intimidating when I wasn't confident about the material. Anything that seemed optional got pushed aside because I was barely managing the required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. The gamified tasks worked for me because there was instant feedback—points, badges, something tangible. I also valued anything hands-on or interactive because that's how my brain works best. If something was just passive reading with no clear outcome, I'd lose interest quickly and feel like I was wasting precious time I didn't have.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With required work already piling up and my completion rate being so low, anything optional got mentally filed under 'nice to have but impossible right now.' Required activities came with the weight of consequences, so they got priority even when I was exhausted. The optional stuff sometimes looked interesting, but my anxiety would tell me I needed to focus on what could actually hurt my grade if I skipped it.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied from printed notes because staring at screens for too long made my anxiety worse. I'd often take handwritten notes while watching videos once, then study from those notes offline for hours. I also used textbooks and external YouTube videos to understand concepts better—sometimes the course explanations didn't click for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through problems on paper or practicing concepts physically—like with flashcards or teaching myself out loud—but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd download a PDF, print it, and spend a whole afternoon marking it up and rewriting key points. My peer interactions number doesn't capture the study sessions I had with classmates in person, working through material together. The LMS only saw me briefly, but my actual study time was much more than what got recorded.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access something once, download it or screenshot the important parts, and then work with it offline for extended periods. My internet access is reliable, but I found it easier to focus without the distraction of being online. I'd watch a video once, take detailed notes, then review those notes multiple times without ever logging back in. The LMS would show one view, but that material might have gotten hours of my attention afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. I was juggling 11 courses and my anxiety was through the roof—I'd have panic attacks just thinking about how behind I was. There were weeks where I could barely bring myself to open my laptop because the notifications and unfinished tasks felt like they were screaming at me. My attendance dropped to almost nothing during those periods because showing up meant confronting how much I was failing. The stress became paralyzing rather than motivating.
Q13: Completely. The workload from having so many courses meant I was constantly triaging—deciding what to sacrifice. Fatigue was huge; I'd stay up late trying to catch up, then be too exhausted to engage meaningfully the next day. Confusion about course material made me avoid the LMS because I didn't want to face content I didn't understand. When other responsibilities came up, the courses that seemed most forgiving got neglected first, and this one often fell into that category.
Q14: All the time. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered because I was too tired to focus. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. There were sessions where I logged in, clicked around, maybe downloaded something, but absorbed absolutely nothing. My brain was present but not engaged—I was physically there but mentally checked out from exhaustion and anxiety.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it hard to start because I was afraid of doing poorly, so I'd avoid the task entirely. I'd tell myself I'd feel more prepared tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel just as overwhelmed. The bigger an assignment seemed, the more I'd procrastinate because breaking it down felt impossible. I knew this was hurting me, but the avoidance felt like the only way to cope with the stress in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me act. I'd ignore something for weeks, then cram desperately in the final hours before it was due. The LMS would show this burst of activity right before due dates and silence otherwise. This pattern was exhausting and meant my work quality suffered, but without that pressure, I couldn't motivate myself. Deadlines created panic, and panic was my primary engine for getting anything done.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it affected me a lot—sometimes positively, sometimes it made things worse. Good feedback gave me a brief confidence boost and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, would send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid that course for a while. I often didn't check feedback promptly because I was scared of what it might say. The delay meant I didn't always apply it to the next assignment in time.
Q18: The course structure with so many components spread across the semester was overwhelming for someone taking 11 courses. I needed clearer priorities—what was essential versus what was supplementary. The gamified elements helped because they broke things into smaller, manageable pieces with immediate rewards. But overall, the structure assumed students had more bandwidth than I actually had, and I often felt lost about where to focus my limited energy.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt achievable and gave me quick wins. What made it harder was when materials were only available online without download options, or when participation required being present at specific times I couldn't manage. Long-form content was difficult for my kinesthetic learning style—I needed more interactive, hands-on activities. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made them feel like a chore rather than genuine learning.
Q20: I think that pattern describes me somewhat. High activity might mean someone is desperately trying to understand or catch up but struggling to produce quality work. They might be anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Or they could be engaging with content but not in ways that translate to completed assignments—like reading and watching but not writing. Sometimes the gap between understanding something and producing work about it is huge.
Q21: This resonates with how I studied offline. Someone could download everything in one session, study extensively from those materials without logging back in, and still learn effectively. They might have strong prior knowledge or efficient study methods that don't require constant LMS interaction. Or they might be getting help from peers, tutors, or external resources that don't show up in the system. Low activity doesn't mean low effort—it might mean effort is happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. They're good at tracking deadlines and submission times. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone actually understood anything. They can't capture anxiety, exhaustion, or the reasons behind behaviors. A log sees actions but not intentions or struggles. It's like judging a book by how many times someone opened it, not whether they read it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections to other things I know. For me, it involves doing something with my hands or actively working through problems, not just passively consuming content. True engagement feels energizing even when it's hard, whereas fake engagement is exhausting and empty. It's about my mind being present and invested, not just my browser being open.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually cared about learning despite my poor metrics. It can't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the anxiety attacks that kept me from logging in, or the genuine effort that went into the little work I did submit. It doesn't show that I passed my exam with a decent score through real understanding, not luck. My circumstances—managing 11 courses with high anxiety—aren't visible in the data. The struggle and the small victories are invisible to the system.
